Partial data point 2026-07-24 (tmp/by-week/2026-W30/external-retrieval-quality-gate.plan.md): when --update --overwrite correctly refused for lack of a security-decisions.log.csv PASS entry, deleting only the specific stale snapshot files first (so they became fresh writes, not overwrites of existing content) and re-running --update --merge restored parity without needing security clearance -- a viable manual recipe for the isolated-file case, though not a fix for the general staleness-detection gap this row is about.

summary: agentteams --update --merge never re-plans the instructions file (copilot-instructions.md / CLAUDE.md-equivalent) for an existing team, regardless of framework -- only fresh generation includes it in the effective output plan. Confirmed two ways: (1) .github/copilot-instructions.md's mtime was untouched by today's --self --update --merge run despite unrelated fenced content elsewhere changing; (2) a --dry-run --json plan for --framework claude --output .claude/agents --update --merge listed 46 entries and none was '../CLAUDE.md', even though the original from-scratch build-log.json's output_files_map explicitly includes it. Any project relying on --update --merge to keep its instructions file current (not just its per-agent files) will silently drift -- exactly the kind of staleness this session had to hand-fix in both .github/agents/orchestrator.agent.md's and .claude/CLAUDE.md's Constitutional Rules sections.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/cli/generate.py or agentteams/render.py (update-mode file planning); output_plan.py

Not fixed here -- flagging as a 4th, not-yet-investigated location for a future pass over agentteams/bridge.py's manifest/report generation to check for the same pattern.

summary: CopilotVSCodeAdapter.vscode_tasks_rel_path() hardcodes "../../.vscode/tasks.json", assuming the agents-dir is always exactly 2 path segments below the true project root (matching the real .github/agents case). When --output points directly at a shallower directory (e.g. this session's own golden-snapshot regen using --output examples/<name>/expected, only 1 segment below its own conceptual project root), the hardcoded offset climbs one level too far and silently writes tasks.json outside the intended output tree entirely (observed: examples/.vscode/tasks.json, sibling to all three example projects, not nested under any one of them). Not fixed here -- discovered incidentally while regenerating example snapshots for BH-11 (blocked nothing, since test_snapshot_comparison only reads *.md/*.svg inside expected/ via rglob); the stray directory was deleted before commit. Same general class as the two absolute-path leaks already fixed this session, but a relative-traversal escape rather than an absolute-path embed.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/frameworks/copilot_vscode.py (vscode_tasks_rel_path); agentteams/vscode_tasks.py (path resolution/join site) -- consider deriving the offset from the actual agents-dir depth rather than a hardcoded constant

summary: Rule S-9's criterion 5 (Pathway Safety Verification, security.template.md) was narrowed today to 'privileged or stateful' external interaction, explicitly excluding routine read-only public-data fetches (the fix for a real conflict with the goose-bridge-entry-actionability fix). @security's re-review flagged one remaining ambiguity this narrowing doesn't resolve: a read-only GET with no write and no persisted session but a real-world cost side effect (e.g. a metered/paid API call) is neither clearly 'privileged/stateful' nor clearly a free inert 'routine public fetch' under the current wording. Not a security hole (no credential/data exposure), but a cost-control gap outside Rule S-9's current stated purpose. Not fixed here -- flagged as a non-blocking follow-up by @security during close-out.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/templates/universal/security.template.md (Rule S-9 criterion 5

summary: OPSEC: .claude/agents/references/memory-index.json (tracked, already committed/pushed in prior history) contains hundreds of absolute filesystem paths with the local OS username, referencing paths across multiple unrelated projects (shared-data, shared-libs, other repos under githubrepositories), not just this project's own path. This project's own path prefix was sanitized in this session's fix-forward commit (1937cbc); the remaining cross-project path variants were not, since a blunt find-replace risks corrupting JSON structure across many differently-shaped absolute paths and deserves a careful, dedicated pass rather than a rushed sed. Git history in this and at least two other repos (researchteam, OrthodoxLLM) retains the original unsanitized values regardless of any working-copy fix -- a full remediation would require history rewrite (filter-repo/BFG), a separate, explicit, user-authorized decision given it rewrites already-pushed public history.
proposed_touch_points: .claude/agents/references/memory-index.json (whatever internal tool/process generates and updates this file -- not found in agentteams/*.py's own source, likely a separate Claude-Code-specific memory/session-index mechanism); git history on affected branches if a rewrite is ever authorized

summary: AUTHORING-GUIDE.md's §6 ("Verify runtime capability before authoring instruction content") doesn't name a specific recurring sub-pattern this session hit twice within goose.py: when a generator function has no manifest/team-composition access (confirmed: _goose_capabilities_content(project_name) and _goosehints_content take no manifest parameter), the correct resolution for team-composition-dependent content is verify-first phrasing ("check whether X before assuming"), not an unconditional assertion, and not a signature change to thread manifest access through. Confirmed twice within goose.py specifically (both this session's pre- and post-implementation audits independently flagged variants of the same issue); the shape is architecturally available to any adapter's extra_output_files generator, not yet observed cross-adapter.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/templates/AUTHORING-GUIDE.md §6 (add named sub-pattern)

summary: A scratch/probe render using a relative --output path can silently overwrite an already-git-tracked directory outside the intended scratch location when the shell's cwd resolves unexpectedly between tool calls. Materialized this session: a subagent's relative --output .claude/agents briefly overwrote this repo's real .claude/agents/ tree with throwaway probe content (self-caught via git status, fully remediated via the tool's own pre-write backup plus a legitimate re-run of the real self-hosting command; independently re-verified byte-for-byte identical to pre-incident state). The CLI accepts a relative --output with no validation or warning even when it resolves onto an existing, git-tracked, non-empty directory.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/cli/generate.py (--output path handling -- require absolute, or warn/refuse when a relative path resolves onto an already-git-tracked directory without --overwrite); agentteams/templates/AUTHORING-GUIDE.md §7 (secondary, defense-in-depth: caution to always use an absolute scratchpad path for exploratory/probe renders)

summary: The CLI's security-intelligence-freshness gate (agentteams/cli/security_gate.py, 24h TTL) blocks an entire generate/update run for ALL output files whenever the cached security snapshot exceeds its TTL with --security-offline and no waiver, even when the files actually being regenerated have zero relationship to security-vulnerability-watch content -- confirmed this session for both --overwrite (fresh generate into examples/*/expected) and --update --merge, forcing a workaround that called ingest/analyze/render/emit directly (mirroring tests/test_integration.py's own _run_pipeline) rather than going through the CLI.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/cli/security_gate.py (gate scope); agentteams/emit.py (write-path integration point) -- scope the freshness gate to only the security-specific output files, or add a lighter per-run bypass

summary: agentteams --update --merge silently does not propagate an edit to an unfenced Invariant Core section to an already-deployed team, with no notice printed anywhere (unlike fenced-content changes, which get an explicit per-file MERGE/UNCHANGED report). Root cause: emit.py's _normalize_generated_content only applies the automatic whole-body default fence when a template declares NO other explicit fence elsewhere in the file. Confirmed by direct count: exactly 5 of 17 domain templates (quality-auditor, research-analyst, retrieval-integrator, technical-validator, tool-doc-researcher) also declare a second explicit fence and are therefore affected -- not "virtually every" template as first characterized during this session's own audit chain (@template-library-expert's diff review), corrected here after an actual count during the retrospective's own adversarial audit. Broadened 2026-07-24 (tmp/by-week/2026-W30/external-retrieval-quality-gate.plan.md's close-out audit): YAML front matter (agents:, tools:, model:, handoffs:) is an even more absolute instance of this same silent-non-propagation problem -- unconditionally excluded from every fence (default whole-body or explicit) for every template, not just the 5 named above. Confirmed via direct reading of emit.py:157-163. Concrete instance: that plan's content-enricher.template.md front-matter change merged correctly into one self-hosted team's body but left another's agents: line stale, undetected until a close-out audit checked front matter specifically. Any fix for the notice-on-non-propagation gap above should cover front matter too, not just unfenced body content.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/emit.py (_normalize_generated_content / per-file entry.action reporting); agentteams/fences.py (per-file diff model) -- surface a notice when a changed template's content falls entirely outside any declared fence, or when a template's front matter differs from an already-generated file's front matter, during --update

summary: agentteams/ingest.py::parse_dependency_manifests/_parse_package_json ingests every package.json dependencies AND devDependencies entry unconditionally as a tools[] entry (category hardcoded 'library'), with no relevance filtering. Most (subject to classify_tool_importance's tier routing) spawn a full references/ref-<tool>-reference.md file. Inflates low-value reference-file volume independent of tmp/by-week/2026-W30/tool-doc-catalog-remediation.plan.md's completion-mechanism fixes -- even with every completion mechanism working perfectly, a devDependency-heavy JS project still generates many low-value files.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/ingest.py (_parse_package_json, parse_dependency_manifests) -- consider deprioritizing/filtering devDependencies relative to dependencies, or gating tools[] inclusion on actual source-code usage (import/require scanning) rather than just manifest presence

The new reference-doc advisory already discloses this inline ('--prune cannot remove them (detection-only today)'); the older, pre-existing agent-file advisory does not carry the equivalent caveat ('Review and delete if obsolete' only) -- the disclosure gap is real but narrower than 'silent,' confined to the older advisory (corrected here after this session's own adversarial retrospective review challenged the original, broader framing).
proposed_touch_points: build_team.py (_prune_removed_files) -- wire both glob-based orphan sets into --prune's deletion-eligible set (same confirmation-gating already in place); OR agentteams/cli/generate.py's older *.agent.md orphan-advisory message -- add the same --prune-cannot-reach-these caveat the newer reference-doc advisory already has, for consistency

Confirmed systemic and pre-existing during tmp/by-week/2026-W30/tool-doc-catalog-remediation.plan.md's post-implementation audit (which found the gap in tool-doc-researcher.template.md specifically) and broadened to the full count during this session's own retrospective adversarial review. Corrected 2026-07-24: research-analyst and tool-doc-researcher dropped from the affected list (count 13→11; denominator unchanged at 24) after tmp/by-week/2026-W30/external-retrieval-quality-gate.plan.md added SECTION MANIFEST blocks to both — confirmed by a direct, self-run count over agentteams/templates/{domain,universal}/*.template.md (24 fenced templates total, 11 missing a manifest), not by re-trusting either post-implementation audit's own restated count (one of which had independently miscounted the denominator as 26).

summary: agentteams/fences.py::_merge_fenced_content cannot insert a new AGENTTEAMS fence at a specific position relative to existing content -- a section_id absent from the on-disk file is always appended at the absolute end of the merged file (confirmed by direct reading, lines 345-351), regardless of where that section_id appears in the fresh template render. This is the shared root cause of a blocking bug and two should-fix findings in tmp/by-week/2026-W30/external-retrieval-quality-gate.plan.md's post-implementation audit: a template author who adds a new fenced section mid-procedure (e.g. a gate step meant to run before an existing hand-off instruction) gets correct placement on fresh generation but silent misplacement -- potentially a direct contradiction in execution order, as it was here -- on --update --merge into an already-generated team. Mitigated in that plan by making the new fenced content self-contained/position-independent (see FENCE-CONVENTIONS.md's new 'Authoring Consequences' section), not by fixing the merge engine itself. Related but distinct from the adjacent 2026-07-23 entry above (emit.py's _normalize_generated_content / silent non-propagation with no notice) -- that entry is about a change to an EXISTING fenced/unfenced region going undetected; this one is about a BRAND-NEW fence landing in the wrong place. Both point at agentteams/fences.py's merge model and may be worth addressing together.
proposed_touch_points: agentteams/fences.py (_merge_fenced_content) -- support an explicit 'insert after fence <section_id>' or 'insert after heading <text>' directive so template authors can control merge-time placement of brand-new fenced sections, instead of requiring every new fence to be written position-independent
